Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Intentionality as the mark of the mental · late 19th – 20th c.

Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Phenomenology and its neighbors ask what consciousness is like from within while refusing to sever experience from embodiment, development, or social life. Brentano’s heirs, Gestalt thinkers, developmental psychologists, and philosophical anthropologists all deepen the analysis of lived form.

1838 – 1917

Franz Brentano

Intentionality as the mark of the mental: the single idea from which modern phenomenology grows.

1848 – 1936

Carl Stumpf

Experimental phenomenology: the psychology of sound and the discipline of describing experience.

1860 – 1944

G. F. Stout

A British Brentanian: presentation, apprehension, and the structure of experience.

1866 – 1938

Kazimierz Twardowski

Content and object in presentations: the Polish school’s founding contribution.

1859 – 1938

Edmund Husserl

Phenomenology as the rigorous science of consciousness in its intentional structure.

1889 – 1976

Martin Heidegger

Being-in-the-world: the existential phenomenology of a being for whom its own being is at issue.

1905 – 1980

Jean-Paul Sartre

Consciousness as a nothingness that is free: imagination, the ego, being and nothingness.

1908 – 1961

Maurice Merleau-Ponty

The body as subject: perception, motility, and the primacy of embodied experience.

1887 – 1967

Wolfgang Köhler

Gestalt form, insight, and a non-reductive philosophy of perception.

1863 – 1931

George Herbert Mead

Self, gesture, and mind as a social process.

1896 – 1980

Jean Piaget

Cognitive development and the construction of mind through action.

1874 – 1928

Max Scheler

Value-feeling, personhood, and the emotional a priori.

1864 – 1944

Jakob von Uexküll

Umwelt, organism, and mind as a world-shaping relation.

1915 – 2016

Jerome Bruner

Meaning-making, development, and the culturally shaped architecture of mind.

1902 – 1977

Alexander Luria

Neuropsychology, culture, and the functional organization of higher mental life.

1858 – 1917

Émile Durkheim

Collective representations, social consciousness, and the mind shaped by the sacred.

1925 – 1961

Frantz Fanon

Embodiment, race, and sociogeny in the making and deformation of consciousness.

1896 – 1934

Lev Vygotsky

Inner speech, mediation, and the social genesis of higher mental functions.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
